title: My process for finding undervalued small-cap stocks
author: agcocom
selftext:
I’m not here to promote stock tickers or hype short-term moves. My goal is simply to share what I’ve learned over time and connect with people who are interested in disciplined trading

Most of my research focuses on small-cap stocks that are undervalued, have relatively low float, and are often ignored by the broader market. Instead of chasing momentum or sudden spikes, I prefer to wait for confirmation and signs that a stock is quietly building a trend

Part of my process involves watching how price behaves around key moving averages such as the 5, 13, 34, and 55 day lines. When price structure, volume, and liquidity begin to align, it can sometimes signal that accumulation is happening before a larger move

This is only one part of my overall workflow, but it helps me filter out random volatility and focus on setups with better structure

Each week I review potential setups, track developments on my watchlist, and share my observations and risk considerations. Everything I share is free. I don’t sell signals, subscriptions, or trading tools

If you’re interested in this kind of research and discussion, feel free to comment or send me a message. I’m always open to sharing ideas and learning from others

comments.author: DesignAnalyst
comments.body:
I'd love to learn more! Also curious about your moving average line choices, specifically the 34 and 55 MA. Why those values? How is a 55 better than the 50? Isn't a 21 better than a 34? 
I have always heard that the conventional wisdom is that you want to focus on moving average lines that the rest of the world is also observing? I recently read somewhere that the 100 MA is rather significant because institutional transactions are made based on price interaction with that MA.

replies.author: agcocom
replies.body:
Basically, 34 and 55 come from the Fibonacci sequence

The idea isn't to follow the crowd but to avoid overly crowded levels like 50 and 200 where everyone's watching , that's why 55 can be better than 50; you get fewer false breakouts and stop hunts. As for 21 vs. 34, sure, 21 is more sensitive and reacts faster, but 34 is smoother. Neither is objectively better , it just depends on whether you want quicker signals or a cleaner trend. 

That said, the widely watched moving averages (50, 100, 200) do matter because institutions actually pay attention to them, especially the 100 MA, which is often used as a trigger for entries

So the smartest approach is to use the 50, 100, and 200 as your core anchors, and then bring in non consensus but effective ones like 34 and 55 as supplements. Using both sets together gives you the most reliable read
